title: Affine Schubert Varieties Are Splinters
speaker: Robert Cass
abstract:
Schubert varieties are known to be Frobenius split in positive characteristic by the work of Mehta and Ramanathan. More recently, Bhatt showed that the full flag variety for GLn
 is a splinter by entirely different methods. In this talk, we will explain these concepts and show how to generalize Bhatt's result to all Schubert varieties for all reductive groups. Our methods apply equally well to affine Schubert varieties, which are related to the Langlands program and to the representation theory of algebraic groups. This is joint work with João Lourenço.

proof_stages.label: Classical Schubert varieties and Frobenius-splitting paradigm
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins with finite flag varieties G/B, Schubert varieties as B-orbit closures, Mehta--Ramanathan Frobenius splitting, and the resulting vanishing and representation-theoretic consequences that serve as the model for the affine story.

proof_stages.label: Affine Schubert varieties and prior singularity results
proof_stages.summary: Affine flag varieties G/B over k((t)), affine Schubert varieties, their arithmetic motivations, and the known Frobenius-splitting/global-F-regularity results are reviewed, including the remaining wild ramification casework.

proof_stages.label: Main splinter theorem and stronger relative form
proof_stages.summary: After recalling Bhatt's non-affine splinter result, the speaker states the joint theorem that normal affine Schubert varieties are splinters, explains the splinter definition, notes the stronger divisor-relative plus-regular form, and uses descent along proper birational maps to justify working on resolutions.

proof_stages.label: Trace-map cohomological criterion for splinters
proof_stages.summary: Using Grothendieck duality for finite maps and Serre duality, the splitting condition for every finite cover is converted into injectivity of a top-cohomology trace map involving the canonical sheaf.

proof_stages.label: Demazure resolution and semiample boundary line bundle
proof_stages.summary: The proof replaces the Schubert variety by its Demazure/Bott--Samelson resolution, computes its Picard group and boundary divisors via its iterated P^1-bundle structure, and isolates the semiample line bundle (1,...,1) while avoiding the older hard descent-to-Schubert-variety argument.

proof_stages.label: Inversion of adjunction with rational boundary
proof_stages.summary: An inversion-of-adjunction theorem for splinter-type properties is applied inductively from a boundary Demazure divisor to the whole resolution, using the absolute integral closure and a rational boundary chosen just below the canonical boundary so that -K minus the boundary is ample.

proof_stages.label: Bhatt finite-cover Kodaira vanishing kills the obstruction
proof_stages.summary: Bhatt's characteristic-p Kodaira vanishing up to finite covers annihilates the cohomology group controlling the trace-map kernel, completing the induction and hence the splinter proof, with global F-regularity-type consequences following afterwards.

proof_stages.label: Mixed-characteristic Witt-vector outlook
proof_stages.summary: The final part discusses Witt-vector affine flag varieties in mixed characteristic, conjectures that their Schubert varieties should be F-nilpotent rather than splinters, relates this to mod-p intersection cohomology, and explains why current inversion-of-adjunction methods are insufficient.

examples.name: Classical GL_n flag variety
examples.note: The case G=GL_n with B upper triangular makes the flag variety concrete as flags in a vector space and identifies the Weyl group with the symmetric group, illustrating the general Schubert-variety definitions.
examples.engagement_reason: GL_n/B is used as the standard flag-variety example and identified with flags/permutation-matrix Weyl representatives, but no calculation or construction is carried out on it.

examples.name: Curves: P^1 and ordinary elliptic curves
examples.note: The speaker uses P^1, smooth projective curves, and ordinary versus supersingular elliptic curves to illustrate how rare Frobenius splitting is and how splinter is stricter than Frobenius split.
examples.engagement_reason: For P^1 the speaker explicitly constructs a Frobenius splitting on the affine chart k[x] and discusses compatibility, and for elliptic curves checks the cohomological obstruction via Frobenius on H^1.

passes.reason: P^1 is the genus-zero minimal highly symmetric curve, while ordinary elliptic curves illustrate that Frobenius-split curves need not be splinters.
